text: His surgical repair included a thoracoscopic approach with multiple trocars placed through and between the ribs to approach the internal organs.
The abdominal exposure was gained only through a thoracoscopic approach, where we were able to elevate the omentum and identify a targeted area of omentum for resection.
The extra lobar sequestration was densely adherent to the edges of the diaphragm.
Initially, we thought that the connecting feeding vessel coming off of the aorta was coming
from the supra-diaphragmatic aorta, but upon more meticulous dissection we identified the aortic vessel to be coming just at the level of the diaphragm.

An endogia curve tip tan load stapler was utilized not only to divide the attachments but also to divide the aortic collateral as well as that portion of the omentum that required full thickness resection.
A combination of a ligature dissection bipolar cautery device and an endoscopic GIA stapler was used to resect and dissect the omentum free.
By stapling and dividing and transecting the omentum, we're able to more clearly identify the pertinent thoracoscopic anatomy.
The omentum was densely adherent to the extralobar and intralobar sequestration, and upon stapling, we identified a bleeding vessel that was not controlled by the stapler.
Instead of addressing this immediately to gain better access to the bleeding vessel, we completed the resection of that portion of the omentum that had herniated through the diaphragm.
We were able to remove this thoracoscopically through one of the separate ports.
We did not worry about placing it into an endo-catch bag, and in fact did not place it into a bag to save money.
We used an endo-loop to control the bleeding vessel as we were able to identify it.

We used different grasping devices to make sure that we did not lose control of the bleeding vessel as we attempted to apply the endo-loop, making sure that at no point in time did we release our hold onto the bleeding vessel attached to the omentum.
By using the endo loop as a snare, we were able to easily control the omental bleeding.
Once this was controlled, the omentum was packed back into the abdomen and we began to identify the extra lobar sequestration coming through the abdomen into the thoracic cavity.

we were able to identify a separate feeding vessel coming directly off of the aorta at the level of the diaphragm, beginning just at the diaphragm and coming superior to the diaphragm to enter into the extra lobar sequestration.
This feeding vessel measured almost a centimeter in size and was far too big to be controlled with clips or an energy device.
Therefore, we used an endogia tan load curve tip stapler to control the vessel that was coming off of the aorta.
The endoscopic endogia vascular stapler was used to staple the aortic collateral and subsequently gain better exposure of the extra lobar sequestration.
It is important to note that this did not have any bronchial connection to the patient's lung.
The remaining portion was en masse ligated, stapled, and divided.
After we completed the extra lobar sequestration resection with an endoscopic stapler, a separate load of the tan load stapler, we completed the first portion of the surgery.
We did note that there was a very large feeding vein coming from a systemic vein for the extra lobar sequestration.

After completion of this, we turned our attention towards identifying, dissecting, encircling, stapling, and dividing the intralobar sequestration as it communicated with the descending thoracic aorta.
This descending thoracic aortic collateral, again, was at least a centimeter in size and could easily be identified as it was retracted superiorly.
To orient the viewers, the head is to the left and the feet are to the right.
The aorta is on the horizontal axis and the stapler is used to divide and separate the intralobar sequestration that was coming off of the basilar segments of the left lower lobe.

we did identify that the intralobar sequestration did not appear to be involved in the superior segment of the left lower lobe and only appeared to be intricately involved in the intralobar communication with the basilar segments of the left lower lobe.
Thus,

we made an attempt to discuss with the patient not only a minimally invasive approach at taking care of his disease, but also a parenchymal sparing approach, trying our best to preserve as much lung parenchyma as we could.
Our second portion of the surgery shows encircling, stapling, and dividing the basilar segmental pulmonary artery.
The endo-GIA curved tip tan load stapler was again used to divide the basilar segmental pulmonary artery.
We specifically dissected and spared the superior segment pulmonary artery branches.
We then used a hook cautery device and a right angle clamp to encircle, staple, and divide the basilar branches going to the left lower lobe
for the bronchus.
The bronchus was controlled with a purple load tri-staple endo-GIA stapler made by Medtronic.
We used a zero silk suture to first encircle the bronchus and then completely remove that suture prior to stapling and dividing it.

Once we had completed this, we then insufflated the lung and used a marker to show the delineation of where the superior segment insufflation and deflation demarcation line between the intersegmental plane should be.
That guided us for our parenchymal intersegmental stapling.
We then encircled, stapled, and divided the left lower lobe pulmonary vein branches going to the basilar segments only.
We specifically preserved the left lower lobe pulmonary vein branches going to the superior segment of the left lower lobe.

By preserving the bronchus, artery, and vein going to the superior segment and then stapling and dividing the intersegmental plane, we were able to perform not only a minimally invasive resection of the intralobar sequestration, but also a parenchymal sparing approach.
Once we had completed our sequestration resection and had adequately controlled the aortic collaterals, we were then taking careful attention to close the diaphragm defect from the bactelec hernia.
A bactelec hernia most likely arises from the posterior left-sided diaphragm.

We placed multiple trans-fascial suture passers utilizing the Carter-Thomason suture passer and then using the endoscopic needle driver and knot pusher with zero and number one proline sutures to repair the diaphragm.
We then re-insufflated the superior segment of the left lower lobe as well as the left upper lobe.
You can see that this patient had a complete fissure between the superior division and the lingula, which is rarely seen.
